era: Rainier Valley, Seattle. Hip-Hop/Radio. E=4 -- broadcast energy. Genre Stage 98. FIRST RADIO PERSONALITY in the engine. New category. KCMU (now KEXP). Fresh Tracks show.

location.formative: Rainier Valley and the Central District -- the base for Seattle hip-hop. KCMU broadcasting from Kane Hall, University District. The geographic divide: hip-hop in Rainier Valley/CD, punk/indie on Capitol Hill.
location.career: Nasty Nes Rodriguez -- DJ, radio host, manager, cultural connector. Fresh Tracks on KCMU. First hip-hop on Seattle radio. Root node of Seattle hip-hop. 1981-1995.

members.name: Nasty Nes Rodriguez
members.note: The first person to play hip-hop on Seattle radio. Fresh Tracks on KCMU. Bridge from New York to Seattle. Manager of early Seattle hip-hop acts. The root node.

associated_musicians.name: Sir Mix-a-Lot
associated_musicians.role: Influenced by the foundation Nasty Nes built
associated_musicians.note: Anthony Ray. 'Posse on Broadway' (1988), 'Baby Got Back' (1992). Came up in the scene Nasty Nes helped create.

associated_musicians.name: Ishmael Butler
associated_musicians.note: Degree 107 (Knife Knights). The root-to-canopy chain: Nasty Nes (root) to Butler (canopy).

associated_musicians.name: Blue Scholars
associated_musicians.note: Geo and Sabzi. Seattle hip-hop. Built on the foundation Nasty Nes laid.

associated_musicians.name: Macklemore & Ryan Lewis
associated_musicians.note: Seattle hip-hop to mainstream. The chain that begins with Nasty Nes.

associated_musicians.name: Common Market
associated_musicians.note: RA Scion and Sabzi. Seattle hip-hop. Same foundation.

theory_nodes.concept: First radio personality in the engine -- category expansion: not a musician but a DJ/radio host/cultural connector; the engine can document the infrastructure that enables music; radio DJs, venue operators, label founders are the hidden architecture of a music scene
examples: Nasty Nes Rodriguez (degree 126) -- first non-musician entry. DJ and radio host.
Fresh Tracks on KCMU -- the first hip-hop on Seattle radio.
The hidden architecture: without Nasty Nes, the Seattle hip-hop trajectory does not exist in the same form.
theory_nodes.description: First radio personality: the engine expands to document music infrastructure, not just musicians.

genealogy.relationship: Category expansion at degree 126. Track whether future non-musician entries follow.

theory_nodes.concept: KCMU-to-KEXP pipeline -- institutional arc: freeform college radio (KCMU, 1972) to global independent powerhouse (KEXP, 2001); Fresh Tracks as early DNA; degrees 126-128 all have radio connections
examples: KCMU: 90.3 FM, Kane Hall, UW. Freeform. No playlists. No advertisers.
Fresh Tracks (Nasty Nes, 1980s): hip-hop on freeform radio. DNA of KEXP's willingness to platform unheard music.
KEXP (2001): rebranded with Paul Allen/EMP support. Global reach. Freeform ethos preserved.
theory_nodes.description: KCMU to KEXP: the institutional arc from basement college station to global influence.

genealogy.relationship: KCMU pipeline begins at degree 126. Track at 127-128.

theory_nodes.concept: Rainier Valley enters the engine -- geographic expansion: southeast Seattle; one of the most diverse corridors in the US; the base for Seattle hip-hop (with Central District); the geography of genre maps to neighborhoods
examples: Rainier Valley: Columbia City, Hillman City, Rainier Beach. Vietnamese, Somali, Ethiopian, Filipino, Mexican, African American communities.
Hip-hop base: Rainier Valley + Central District. Punk/indie base: Capitol Hill. Geography of genre.
First Rainier Valley entry in the engine.
theory_nodes.description: Rainier Valley: new neighborhood. The base for Seattle hip-hop. Geographic expansion.

genealogy.relationship: Rainier Valley enters at degree 126. Track neighborhood connections.

cross_references.relationship: ENERGY DROP. E=7 (TAAS, post-hardcore, Capitol Hill) to E=4 (Nasty Nes, hip-hop/radio, Rainier Valley). New maximum followed by broadcast moderation. Different genres, neighborhoods, eras.
HIP-HOP CONNECTION. Sabzi (Blue Scholars, Common Market). Nasty Nes laid the foundation that made Blue Scholars possible. Root node (1980s) to branches (2000s).
ROOT TO CANOPY. Ishmael Butler (Shabazz Palaces/Digable Planets) + Erik Blood. E=6. Nasty Nes (root) to Butler (canopy). 1980s radio to 2010s experimental hip-hop.
GEOGRAPHIC CONTRAST. Capitol Hill (post-hardcore) vs. Rainier Valley (hip-hop/radio). Two neighborhoods, two genres, two roles in Seattle music history.
INFRASTRUCTURE COMPARISON. Kill Rock Stars (label, Olympia) vs. KCMU (station, Seattle). Two nodes of independent music infrastructure. Both enable music commercial systems would never support.
